findings.topic: Android memory-safety trend as memory-safe code share grew
findings.summary: Google's Android security data is the strongest production evidence for the 'new code in memory-safe language' strategy. Memory-safety vulnerabilities as a share of total Android vulns fell from 76% (2019) to 35% (2022) to 24% (2024) - well below the ~70% industry norm. Absolute counts: annual memory-safety vulns dropped from 223 (2019) to 85 (2022); 2024 extrapolated to 36 (27 after Sept bulletin). Driver: Android began shifting NEW development to memory-safe languages (~2019). By Android 13, a majority of new code is memory-safe and ~21% of all new native (C/C++/Rust) code was Rust, with ~1.5M LOC Rust in AOSP (Keystore2, UWB stack, DNS-over-HTTP3, Virtualization framework AVF). To date ZERO memory-safety vulns discovered in Android Rust code despite that volume; Google estimates Rust already prevented hundreds of vulns (historical density >1 vuln/kLOC in C/C++ media/BT/NFC). Memory-safety bugs were 86% of critical-severity and 89% of remotely-exploitable Android vulns in 2022, and 78% of in-the-wild exploited vulns - i.e. the class you eliminate is the worst class. Key mechanism (2024 post): vulns decay exponentially (have a half-life); 5-year-old code has 3.4x-7.4x lower vuln density than new code, so prioritizing new-code safety beats rewrites.
findings.designImplication: Validates the core TABOS bet: write the frozen capability microkernel and all NEW system code (services, runtime, SDKs) in a memory-safe language (Rust) rather than C/C++. Do NOT plan mass rewrites of any imported C - the payoff is front-loaded on new code. The <=15 kSLOC frozen kernel written in safe-by-default Rust plausibly eliminates ~70-80% of the vuln class that dominates OS security incidents.

findings.topic: Rust for Linux: merge status, subsystems, Binder and Nova drivers
findings.summary: Rust first merged into mainline Linux in v6.1 (Dec 2022) as infrastructure; it is now an established, multi-subsystem effort. The Android Binder driver (one of the most security/performance-critical Android components, carrying the majority of Android IPC) was rewritten in Rust and merged into Linux v6.18-rc1 - originally authored by Wedson Almeida Filho, now maintained by Alice Ryhl. The Nova GPU driver (Rust successor to Nouveau for GSP-based NVIDIA RTX20/Turing+ GPUs) has parts in mainline at drivers/gpu/nova-core/ and drivers/gpu/drm/nova/, developed in drm-rust-next. Other in-mainline Rust users: AMCC QT2025 PHY, ASIX PHY, DRM Panic QR generator, Null Block driver, Tyr GPU driver. Governance (rust-for-linux.com/rust-kernel-policy): per-subsystem adoption is opt-in; the 'RUST' subsystem maintains only core facilities and acts as fallback maintainer; subsystems may temporarily allow C changes to break Rust builds ('stage 1' as block layer did) and duplicate C/Rust 'reference drivers' to bootstrap. At least 6+ public FTEs across major companies work on RfL.
findings.designImplication: Rust at the deepest privileged layer of a 30M-LOC production kernel is now normalized, not experimental - de-risks choosing Rust for the TABOS microkernel and node-image drivers (vLLM/llama.cpp engines behind Rust driver shims). The reference-driver / staged-integration model is a usable template for letting TABOS scheme-provider daemons and tool drivers land incrementally without freezing the kernel ABI.

findings.topic: Linux maintainer endorsements (Greg KH, Torvalds engagement)
findings.summary: Greg Kroah-Hartman (stable-tree maintainer, sees ~every kernel CVE for 15+ years), Feb 19 2025 on lore.kernel.org: 'The majority of bugs (quantity, not quality/severity) we have are due to the stupid little corner cases in C that are totally gone in Rust. Things like simple overwrites of memory... error path cleanups, forgetting to check error values, and use-after-free mistakes... these types of issues just go away.' He adds Rust lets the kernel 'define our in-kernel apis in ways that make them almost impossible to get wrong,' while noting 'we have 30 million lines of C code that isn't going anywhere any year soon.' The 'Rust kernel policy' thread (Feb-Mar 2025) drew 183+ messages with Linus Torvalds personally posting 29 times defending Rust integration against C++-alternative proposals.
findings.designImplication: Independent confirmation from the people who triage real OS bugs at scale: Rust removes the high-frequency, low-glory error classes (UAF, error-path leaks, unchecked returns) that consume maintainer review bandwidth. For TABOS this argues for Rust-typed capability/evaluator APIs in the kernel so misuse is a compile error, not a review burden - directly supports a frozen, hard-to-misuse kernel surface.

findings.topic: Microsoft Windows kernel Rust (win32kbase_rs) and Azure stance
findings.summary: Rust now ships IN the production Windows kernel. The GDI REGION data type was reimplemented in Rust as win32kbase_rs.sys; Check Point Research confirmed it running in Windows 11 24H2 builds (10.0.26100.3037, module ~148KB->164KB after a May 28 2025 KB5058499 fix at 10.0.26100.4202). David 'dwizzle' Weston (Dir. OS Security) at BlueHat IL 2023: Windows would boot with Rust in the kernel 'in the next several weeks or months,' converting internal C++ data types to Rust; there is now a Windows kernel syscall implemented in Rust. DWriteCore: ~152,000 lines Rust + ~96,000 lines C++, with 5-15% faster glyph shaping. Win32 GDI port: ~36,000 lines Rust, passes all GDI tests, boots in Win11 (behind a feature flag). Drivers: ~70% of Microsoft's CVEs since 2006 are memory-safety issues. Azure CTO Mark Russinovich (Sept 2022): 'it's time to halt starting any new projects in C/C++ and use Rust... the industry should declare those languages as deprecated.' Caveat: Check Point found a Rust GDI bug where a bounds-check panic deliberately BSODs - MSRC called it correct behavior ('the Rust code correctly catches an out-of-bounds array access and triggers a panic'); CP argues a failed safety check should not crash the system.
findings.designImplication: Both hyperscaler-OS vendors (Google, Microsoft) ship Rust in privileged kernel code - strong precedent for the TABOS kernel language choice. The DWrite/GDI data points show Rust matches or beats C++ on perf, defusing the 'safety costs speed' objection for kernel-class code. The BSOD caveat is a concrete engineering standard for TABOS: in a microkernel holding evaluators, panic-on-violation must NOT be the default availability story - design fallible APIs (Result) and graceful capability-denial paths rather than aborting, especially for attacker-reachable input.

findings.topic: AWS Firecracker + Google crosvm - Rust VMMs in production (TABOS's exact substrate class)
findings.summary: The two reference KVM-based microVM monitors that TABOS's node image targets are both Rust. Firecracker (AWS, Apache-2.0) is a minimalist Rust VMM on KVM purpose-built for serverless; per the NSDI'20 paper it runs in AWS Lambda and Fargate supporting 'millions of production workloads, and trillions of requests per month.' Built-in isolation: thread-specific seccomp filters + a Jailer that applies cgroup/namespace isolation then drops privileges; defaults to 1 vCPU / 128 MiB, demand fault paging and CPU oversubscription. crosvm (Google, BSD-3) is 'a secure, lightweight, and performant VMM written in Rust,' originally for ChromeOS (Crostini) and Android (ARCVM), now also Cuttlefish and Windows. crosvm enforces a process-per-device model: each virtio device is forked into its own Minijail sandbox (VFS pivot_root, PID/User/Network namespaces, strict seccomp-BPF, all capabilities dropped). Supports x86_64/aarch64/riscv64 and KVM plus Gunyah/GenieZone/WHPX/HAXM hypervisors.
findings.designImplication: TABOS's chosen substrate is already a memory-safe Rust ecosystem at planetary scale - building the node image and userspace daemons in Rust gives language/toolchain coherence with the VMM layer (shared crates: kvm-ioctls, vm-memory, virtio bindings from rust-vmm). crosvm's process-per-device + Minijail + seccomp model is a directly reusable blueprint for isolating TABOS scheme-provider daemons (memory:, model:, tool:) and WASM nanoprocess hosts. Standard to adopt: per-device/per-daemon seccomp-BPF allowlists and namespace/jailer drop-privilege boot, matching Firecracker's Jailer.

findings.topic: Asterinas framekernel (arXiv:2506.03876) - minimal sound TCB via unsafe isolation
findings.summary: Asterinas (submitted 4 Jun 2025; arXiv ID confirmed 2506.03876) directly addresses the central problem of Rust OS design: 'Existing Rust-based OSes fall short due to their improper use of unsafe Rust in kernel development.' It proposes the 'framekernel' architecture for intra-kernel privilege separation. Concrete results: a Linux ABI-compatible kernel implemented ENTIRELY in safe Rust on top of a small framework called OSTD, supporting 200+ Linux syscalls (210+ stated), delivering 'performance on par with Linux,' while confining the memory-safety Trusted Computing Base (the unsafe-containing core) to only ~14.0% of the codebase. Authors are an industrial/academic team (incl. Ant Group). This is the academic formalization of how to keep Rust's safety guarantee meaningful in a kernel: all unsafe lives in a small, audited framework; the bulk of the kernel is provably safe Rust that cannot violate memory safety even with bugs.
findings.designImplication: This is the most decision-relevant single paper for TABOS's <=15 kSLOC frozen kernel. Adopt the framekernel/OSTD pattern: concentrate ALL unsafe Rust (raw MMIO, page tables, capability primitives, evaluator intrinsics) into a tiny audited foundation crate; write everything else - capability logic, evaluators' policy, scheme dispatch - in safe Rust that the compiler proves cannot corrupt memory. Make 'TCB = % of code containing unsafe' an explicit, tracked engineering metric (target on the order of Asterinas's ~14%, ideally lower given a 15 kSLOC budget) and gate releases on it.

findings.topic: Redox OS - Rust microkernel with scheme-based services (direct architectural analog)
findings.summary: Redox is a complete Unix-like MICROKERNEL OS written in Rust, active since 2015. Its design is a near-twin of TABOS's stated architecture: microkernel core, drivers in userspace, and - critically - 'OS Services provided via Schemes.' Redox 'schemes' are URL-like namespaced resource providers (e.g. file:, disk:, tcp:) resolved by userspace daemons - structurally identical to TABOS's memory:/model:/tool: scheme-provider daemons. Other features: kernel and drivers in Rust, custom Rust libc (relibc), full-disk-encryption boot, NVMe/SATA/USB boot, Intel/AMD + ARM + RISC-V, and it is an official Rust Tier 2/3 platform target with std support. Minimal image <50MB; desktop variant <512MB RAM. Predominantly MIT-licensed.
findings.designImplication: Redox is prior art that the exact TABOS shape (Rust microkernel + userspace scheme-provider daemons) is buildable and has run for a decade - strong feasibility signal and a source of reusable patterns (scheme resolution, userspace driver IPC, relibc). Worth studying its scheme IPC ABI before freezing TABOS's kernel<->daemon interface. Confirms Rust std/Tier-2 support is mature enough that TABOS userspace daemons can use the standard library.

findings.topic: Theseus, Tock, Hermit - Rust OS research lineage (microkernel, embedded, unikernel)
findings.summary: Three peer-reviewed Rust OS efforts spanning TABOS's relevant form factors. (1) Theseus (Boos et al., OSDI'20, USENIX): a from-scratch Rust OS using an 'intralingual' approach that shifts OS responsibilities to the compiler via language-level mechanisms, enabling live evolution and fault recovery of core components - demonstrates Rust's type system enforcing OS-semantic invariants. (2) Tock (Levy et al., SOSP'17): a Rust embedded OS for Cortex-M/RISC-V where the Rust-written kernel isolates capsule drivers from each other at compile time and uses MPUs to isolate apps; the SOSP'25 retrospective is titled 'Tock: From Research To Securing 10 Million Computers' - i.e. 10M+ production deployments. Tock's papers also candidly document Rust's limits for embedded kernels (PLOS'15 'Ownership is Theft,' EuroSec'22 tiered-trust threat model). (3) Hermit (RWTH-Aachen): a Rust unikernel (rewrite of C-based HermitCore) targeting KVM VMs and HPC/cloud; kernel and Rust-runtime integration are 100% Rust with no C/C++; unmodified Rust apps run on it. Active research through 2025 (PLOS'25 paper on sandboxed unikernel extensions, incl. WASM/browser-to-kernel sandboxing).
findings.designImplication: Covers all three TABOS layers with production/academic precedent: Theseus -> compiler-enforced invariants for the frozen microkernel and live component evolution; Tock -> compile-time driver isolation + MPU app isolation (10M-device proof) for the capability/driver model; Hermit -> a Rust unikernel-on-KVM, exactly TABOS's 'unikernel-style node image on Firecracker-class VMM' - and its PLOS'25 work on WASM-sandboxed unikernel extensions maps onto the TABOS WASM nanoprocess runtime. Read Tock's threat-model docs as a template for writing TABOS's own.

findings.topic: Google KataOS / Sparrow - Rust userspace on the seL4 microkernel
findings.summary: Google Research (Oct 2022) open-sourced KataOS, a secure OS for embedded ML, 'implemented almost entirely in Rust' on top of the seL4 microkernel - chosen because seL4 is 'mathematically proven secure, with guaranteed confidentiality, integrity, and availability.' Released components: the sel4-sys crate (seL4 syscall API in Rust), an alternate rootserver written in Rust for dynamic system-wide memory management, and seL4 kernel modifications to reclaim rootserver memory; KataOS can dynamically load/run third-party apps outside the static CAmkES framework. Sparrow is the reference platform combining KataOS with an OpenTitan RISC-V root-of-trust (initial release targeted 64-bit ARM under QEMU). Rust is credited with eliminating 'entire classes of bugs, such as off-by-one errors and buffer overflows.'
findings.designImplication: Demonstrates the exact split TABOS proposes - a small high-assurance microkernel (here formally-verified seL4 in C, with hardware-enforced capabilities) plus Rust userspace services and dynamically-loaded third-party apps. If TABOS wants stronger-than-Rust kernel assurance for the evaluator core, seL4's capability model + formal proofs is the benchmark; the sel4-sys/rootserver-in-Rust pattern shows how to keep the verified kernel tiny while building everything above it in safe Rust. Note: verify current maintenance status of KataOS/Sparrow (the AmbiML repos saw limited activity after the 2022 announcement) before depending on the code.

findings.topic: How much unsafe Rust is used in practice (quantified)
findings.summary: Multiple measurements converge: unsafe is rare but its safety obligations propagate, so it must be isolated and audited. Rust Foundation (May 2024, via their 'Painter' tool over crates.io): of ~127,000 crates with significant code, 24,362 (19.11%) use the 'unsafe' keyword and 34.35% make a direct call into another crate that uses unsafe; the heaviest unsafe user is the 'windows' crate (FFI into Win32 APIs) - i.e. most unsafe is FFI into non-Rust code, not gratuitous. Evans et al. (ICSE'20 / arXiv:2007.00752): the keyword unsafe appears in <30% of Rust libraries, but >50% of libraries cannot be fully statically checked because unsafe is reachable somewhere in the call chain - 'propagation of unsafeness' is the real challenge. Production data point: Android's entire new UWB stack had exactly TWO uses of unsafe (both for crossing the Rust/Java JNI boundary), and that scrutiny surfaced a race condition. Tooling for auditing unsafe: Miri (UB checker), plus Foundation tools Painter/TypoMania/Sandpit.
findings.designImplication: Sets the engineering standard for TABOS: (1) treat unsafe as a budgeted, reviewed resource - track total unsafe blocks and forbid unsafe outside a small kernel-foundation crate (#![forbid(unsafe_code)] on all higher layers, mirroring Asterinas's framekernel and Tock's capsule model); (2) because unsafeness propagates through call chains, audit transitive unsafe (cargo-geiger) and run Miri in CI on the foundation crate; (3) expect most unsafe to live at FFI seams (the vLLM/llama.cpp/CUDA driver shims, MMIO, hypercalls) - concentrate review there. The <=15 kSLOC frozen kernel should aim for an unsafe surface far below the 19% ecosystem average.

openQuestions: Current maintenance status of Google KataOS/Sparrow (AmbiML) repos - the 2022 open-source release saw limited follow-through; confirm before depending on sel4-sys or the Rust rootserver code.

Exact count and list of Linux subsystems with merged Rust as of mid-2026 (this report confirms Binder in v6.18-rc1, Nova, PHY drivers, null_blk, DRM panic, Tyr) - a current MAINTAINERS/LWN Rust-index pull would give the precise tally.

Whether TABOS's frozen microkernel should sit on a formally-verified C microkernel (seL4, KataOS precedent) for proof-grade assurance vs. a pure-safe-Rust framekernel (Asterinas precedent, ~14% unsafe TCB) - a fundamental assurance-vs-coherence tradeoff not resolvable from these sources alone.

Engineering-standard target for TABOS's unsafe budget: Asterinas hit ~14.0% memory-safety TCB; ecosystem average is ~19% of crates touching unsafe - what concrete per-release unsafe-LOC ceiling and CI gate (Miri, cargo-geiger) should the 15 kSLOC kernel commit to?

Performance parity evidence is OS-specific (Asterinas 'on par with Linux'; Windows DWrite +5-15%) - TABOS needs its own benchmark plan for the evaluator hot path before assuming Rust imposes no overhead vs. C for capability checks.

findings.topic: NSA Software Memory Safety CSI — recommended-languages list (Nov 2022, updated Apr 2023 v1.1)
findings.summary: National Security Agency Cybersecurity Information Sheet 'Software Memory Safety' (doc U/OO/219936-22, PP-23-0782; original 10 Nov 2022, fetched copy footer 'APR 2023 Ver. 1.1'). Verbatim recommendation: 'NSA recommends using a memory safe language when possible.' Path-forward: 'NSA advises organizations to consider making a strategic shift from programming languages that provide little or no inherent memory protection, such as C/C++ and assembly, to a memory safe language when possible.' Explicit MSL list given twice: 'Python, Java, C#, Go, Delphi/Object Pascal, Swift, Ruby, Rust, and Ada.' Cites the Microsoft 2019 stat that 'from 2006 to 2018 70 percent of their vulnerabilities were due to memory safety issues' and a similar Google/Chrome figure. Notes MSLs carry GC and bounds-check performance/flexibility costs and that 'unsafe' escape hatches and FFI to non-MSL libraries localize but do not remove risk.
findings.designImplication: This is the foundational US-gov list TABOS will be measured against. Of the nine NSA-blessed MSLs, only Rust is non-GC and viable for a <=15 kSLOC frozen capability microkernel; Go/Java/C#/Python/Ruby/Swift/Delphi/Ada(GC modes) carry runtimes/GC unsuitable for kernel core. C/C++/assembly are explicitly named as the languages to shift AWAY from. Adopt Rust for kernel + safety-critical evaluators; reserve C/C++ only behind driver boundaries (vLLM/llama.cpp), matching NSA's 'localize where memory problems could exist' framing.

findings.topic: CISA-led 'The Case for Memory Safe Roadmaps' (6 Dec 2023) — multinational joint guidance + MSL appendix
findings.summary: Title: 'The Case for Memory Safe Roadmaps: Why Both C-Suite Executives and Technical Experts Need to Take Memory Safe Coding Seriously.' Publish date 6 Dec 2023. Authoring agencies on every page footer: 'CISA | NSA | FBI | ASD's ACSC | CCCS | NCSC-UK | NCSC-NZ | CERT-NZ' (US + Australia + Canada + UK + New Zealand). Urges 'senior executives at every software manufacturer to reduce customer risk by prioritizing design and development practices that implement MSLs' and to 'create and publish memory safe roadmaps.' Roadmap mandated elements: (1) Defined phases with dates and outcomes; (2) 'Date for MSLs in new systems. Publish the date after which the company will write new code solely in an MSL'; (3) internal developer training/integration plan; (4) external dependency plan for C/C++ libraries/OSS; (5) transparency plan (quarterly/semi-annual updates); (6) CVE support program plan. Appendix 'Memory Safe Languages' profiles C#, Go, Java, Python, Rust, Swift. On Rust: ownership model + 'borrow checker… enforces correctness at compile time to prevent memory safety and concurrency problems at runtime' and notes Rust adoption in 'the Linux kernel, Android, and Windows.'
findings.designImplication: TABOS should pre-emptively publish a memory-safe roadmap as a product artifact: declare a public 'new code solely in an MSL (Rust)' date, an external-dependency plan for the C/C++ engines (vLLM/llama.cpp) and OSS deps, and a CVE/CWE program. The appendix singling out Rust for kernel-class work (Linux/Android/Windows) is direct precedent legitimizing a Rust microkernel. The six-element roadmap doubles as an engineering-standards checklist TABOS can adopt verbatim.

findings.topic: CISA/FBI 'Product Security Bad Practices' (v1.0 Oct 2024; v2.0 Jan 2025) — memory-unsafe new products = #1 bad practice, roadmap deadline end-2025
findings.summary: Joint CISA + FBI guidance. Change record: v1.0 October 2024 (initial); v2.0 January 2025 ('Additional context added to the memory safety section' plus new bad practices). Bad Practice #1 (Product Properties): 'The development of new product lines for use in service of critical infrastructure or NCFs in a memory-unsafe language (e.g., C or C++) where readily available alternative memory-safe languages could be used is dangerous and significantly elevates risk to national security…' For existing products: 'not having a published memory safety roadmap is dangerous…' Recommended action (verbatim): 'Software manufacturers should develop new product lines in memory safe languages. For existing products, software manufacturers should publish a memory safety roadmap by the end of 2025.' Carve-out: 'Publication of a memory safety roadmap does not apply to products that have an announced end-of-support date that is prior to Jan. 1, 2030.' Mapped to CWE-119. Document is explicitly 'non-binding… imposes no requirement.' Adjacent bad practices relevant to an OS: default passwords (CWE-1392/1393), SQLi (CWE-89), OS command injection (CWE-78), shipping with KEV-listed components, hardcoded credentials, deprecated crypto (TLS1.0/1.1, MD5, SHA-1, DES) + 'begin supporting standardized post-quantum cryptographic algorithms.'
findings.designImplication: This is the sharpest 'industrial standard' pressure: a greenfield OS in C/C++ is named the #1 bad practice. Because TABOS is greenfield, building the kernel/core in Rust lets it claim full compliance with the strongest clause ('develop new product lines in memory safe languages') rather than merely the weaker roadmap path. The end-2025 roadmap deadline and Jan-1-2030 EOL carve-out set the calendar for any C/C++ retained components. The crypto clause means TABOS should plan PQC-ready, modern-TLS-only crypto from day one (relevant to the memory-safe crypto-library gap noted elsewhere).

findings.topic: White House ONCD 'Back to the Building Blocks' (Feb 2024) — exact recommendation + the space/kernel carve-out
findings.summary: Office of the National Cyber Director technical report 'Back to the Building Blocks: A Path Toward Secure and Measurable Software', February 2024 (companion 'memory safety fact sheet' dated 26 Feb 2024). Core recommendation: 'the most effective ways to address those issues is by examining the programming language itself… adopting memory safe programming languages.' Cites 'up to 70 percent of security vulnerabilities in memory unsafe languages patched and assigned a CVE designation are due to memory safety issues' and 'When large code bases are migrated to a memory safe language… memory safety vulnerabilities are nearly eliminated.' Critically defines a kernel/embedded carve-out: space/embedded systems require a language that (1) lets code be 'close to the kernel so that it can tightly interact with both software and hardware'; (2) 'support determinism so the timing of the outputs are consistent'; and (3) 'must not have — or be able to override — the garbage collector.' States 'the most widely used languages that meet all three properties are C and C++, which are not memory safe… Rust… has the three requisite properties above, but has not yet been proven in space systems.' Offers complementary hardware (Arm MTE, CHERI/Morello) and formal methods (sound static analysis, model checkers, assertion-based testing) as alternative paths where MSLs are infeasible.
findings.designImplication: This document hands TABOS its exact technical argument: of all MSLs, ONLY Rust satisfies the close-to-kernel + deterministic + no-GC trifecta — disqualifying Go/Java/C#/Python for the microkernel and validating Rust as the kernel language. The 'not yet proven' caveat is a known risk to address via the complementary controls ONCD endorses: pair the Rust kernel with hardware memory tagging (MTE/CHERI where available) and formal methods on the frozen evaluators (<=15 kSLOC is small enough to formally verify, seL4-style). The three CxO-dimension model (developer process / analysis-testing / execution environment) maps onto TABOS's WASM-nanoprocess sandboxing as the 'resilient execution environment' dimension.

findings.topic: DARPA TRACTOR (Translating All C to Rust) — federal R&D betting on Rust as the C successor
findings.summary: DARPA program 'Translating All C to Rust (TRACTOR)'. Stated goal: 'automate the translation of legacy C code to Rust… to achieve the same quality and style that a skilled Rust developer would produce, thereby eliminating the entire class of memory safety security vulnerabilities present in C programs.' Method: 'novel combinations of software analysis, such as static analysis and dynamic analysis, and machine learning techniques like large language models.' Test/eval by 'a team at MIT Lincoln Laboratory'; benchmarks/tools published at ll.mit.edu/tractor. Program announcement 'Eliminating Memory Safety Vulnerabilities Once and For All' (31 Jul 2024), PM Dr. Dan Wallach: 'Rust forces the programmer to get things right… the rules give you freedom. They're like guardrails.' Proposers Day 26 Aug 2024. Frames memory-safety bugs as 'the most prevalent type of disclosed software vulnerability.'
findings.designImplication: DARPA naming Rust specifically (not 'a memory-safe language' generically) as the destination for all legacy C confirms Rust is the government-anointed systems-programming target — de-risking TABOS's Rust kernel bet against the 'pick a different MSL' alternative. TRACTOR's LLM-assisted C→Rust tooling is directly usable for TABOS driver work: wrapping/porting C/C++ engine glue (llama.cpp, CUDA shims) toward Rust over time. Track ll.mit.edu/tractor benchmarks as off-the-shelf migration tooling.

findings.topic: EU Cyber Resilience Act (Reg. (EU) 2024/2847) — an OS + hypervisor product is a regulated 'important product'
findings.summary: CRA entered into force 10 Dec 2024; main manufacturer obligations apply from 11 Dec 2027; vulnerability/incident reporting obligations apply from 11 Sep 2026. Mandatory cybersecurity requirements span 'planning, design, development and maintenance'; CE marking required; some products need third-party (notified body) conformity assessment. Penalties: non-compliance with essential cybersecurity requirements (Annex I) 'subject to administrative fines of up to EUR 15 000 000 or, if the offender is an undertaking, up to 2,5 % of the its total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year, whichever is higher.' Reporting cadence to ENISA/CSIRT: early-warning of an actively exploited vulnerability or severe incident 'within 24 hours' of awareness; fuller notification 'within 72 hours'; final report 'no later than 14 days after a corrective or mitigating measure is available.' CRITICAL for TABOS: Annex III 'Important Products with Digital Elements — Class I' explicitly lists item 11 'Operating systems' (also boot managers #8, network management #6, SIEM #7, identity/PAM #1). Annex III Class II lists 'Hypervisors and container runtime systems that support virtualised execution of operating systems and similar environments' and 'Firewalls, intrusion detection and prevention systems.' Important products that don't fully apply harmonised standards must undergo EU-type examination (module B) or full-quality-assurance (module H) assessment.
findings.designImplication: TABOS is squarely in CRA's stricter tier on multiple counts: the OS image is Annex III Class I; its Firecracker/KVM-class VMM and WASM nanoprocess runtime read as Class II 'hypervisors and container runtime systems.' Selling into the EU therefore demands documented secure-SDLC, SBOM, CE marking, and the 24h/72h/14-day vulnerability-reporting machinery — wired in before 11 Sep 2026 (reporting) / 11 Dec 2027 (full). Engineering-standards choice: adopt the forthcoming CRA harmonised standards / EN 18031-class controls to avoid mandatory notified-body assessment; the 15M-EUR / 2.5%-turnover fine makes this a board-level, not optional, constraint. Memory-safe language choice also feeds CRA Annex I 'no known exploitable vulnerabilities' / secure-by-default essential requirements.

findings.topic: CISA Secure by Design Pledge (launched 1 May 2024) — 200+ signatories, memory-safe roadmap is a named pledge action
findings.summary: Voluntary pledge for enterprise software products/services, launched 1 May 2024, structured around seven goals; 'over 200 software manufacturers' / 'hundreds of companies' have signed (per CISA's own pages). Goal 3 ('Reducing entire classes of vulnerability') names memory safety explicitly; example approaches: 'Developing a memory safe roadmap to transition to memory safe languages in a prioritized approach and writing new products in memory safe languages' and 'Providing secure defaults for developers, such as… building blocks of secure functions and libraries that make it impossible (or significantly more difficult) to introduce a certain class of vulnerability.' Measurable-progress example: 'Publishing a memory safety roadmap.' Other six goals: MFA (incl. phishing-resistant), eliminating default passwords, security-patch uptake, published VDP, CWE/CPE in every CVE record + timely CVEs, customer evidence-of-intrusion logging (>=6 months retention for SaaS). Explicitly 'voluntary and not legally binding'; 'CISA does not enforce nor verify adherence.'
findings.designImplication: The pledge converts the memory-safe roadmap and 'secure building-block libraries' into a concrete, widely-adopted industry checklist TABOS can adopt as its public engineering-standards baseline. The 'building blocks of secure functions/libraries that make it impossible to introduce a class of vulnerability' goal maps directly onto TABOS's capability-microkernel + WASM-nanoprocess design philosophy (capabilities/sandboxing as structural vuln-class elimination). Aligning the project from inception with all seven goals (MFA-by-default, no default passwords, VDP, full CWE/CPE CVE hygiene, audit logging) is cheap pre-launch and signals parity with 200+ peers.

findings.topic: Signatory real-world MSL commitments — Rust-first precedents and the crypto-library FFI gap
findings.summary: CISA's progress-reports page links self-published implementations. Google ('7 ways we're incorporating security by design', white paper 'An Overview of Google's Commitment to Secure by Design'): reduces 'entire classes of vulnerabilities including cross-site scripting (XSS), SQL injection (SQLi), memory safety issues' via its 'Safe Coding framework.' Beyond Identity ('Getting Rusty: Beyond Identity's Journey to Memory Safety', Crispin Cowan): Rust-first since 2020, after first trialing Go and rejecting it because 'the Go runtime hijacking exceptions' could leave 'sensitive data in memory where the stack used to be'; chose Rust because 'the Rust runtime is much leaner than Go's runtime, essentially just a thread scheduler' and lacks exception handling. Key cautions for a systems product: cross-platform host-OS integration required hand-written 'Rust→C Foreign Function Interfaces (FFIs)'; library gaps mean FFI to memory-unsafe code (so 'there is code inside the application that is not guaranteed to be memory-safe'); and compliance-grade crypto forces FFI 'to memory-unsafe libraries like OpenSSL… Something akin to Heartbleed could still impact such products… it is not possible to create an application that is both memory-safe and crypto-compliance conforming' until certified memory-safe crypto exists. Microsoft published 'Microsoft's Secure by Design journey: One year of success' (17 Apr 2025).
findings.designImplication: Concrete confirmation that Rust (not GC'd MSLs) is the systems-software choice peers converge on — and a precise warning list for TABOS: (1) the kernel/driver FFI boundary to C/C++ engines (vLLM/llama.cpp/CUDA) is the residual memory-unsafe surface and must be capability-isolated/sandboxed; (2) crypto is the hardest memory-safe gap — prefer Rust-native, audited crypto (rustls, aws-lc-rs/ring) over OpenSSL FFI where FIPS/cert constraints allow, and document the residual risk where they don't; (3) Go's runtime/exception behavior is a documented anti-pattern for low-level/secret-handling code, reinforcing Rust over Go even for userspace scheme-provider daemons that touch secrets.

findings.topic: CISA/NSA 'Memory Safe Languages: Reducing Vulnerabilities in Modern Software Development' (23 Jun 2025) — adoption-obstacles guidance
findings.summary: Newest joint CISA + NSA guide, published 23 Jun 2025 (CSI at media.defense.gov/2025/Jun/23/2003742198). Per CISA's landing page it 'identifies the main obstacles in adopting memory safe languages, provides practical solutions to address these challenges, and emphasizes critical factors for organizations aiming to shift towards more secure software development.' Expands recommendations for critical-infrastructure orgs across four pillars: 'language-level protections, library support, robust tooling, and developer training.' This is the 2025 successor/companion to the 2022 NSA CSI and 2023 Case-for-Memory-Safe-Roadmaps, showing sustained, escalating US-gov pressure rather than a one-off.
findings.designImplication: The four named pillars (language-level protections, library support, tooling, developer training) are exactly the engineering-standards axes TABOS must resource for a Rust codebase: invest in a curated internal crate/library set (covering the library-support gap Beyond Identity flagged), CI tooling (cargo-audit, MIRI, clippy, fuzzing, formal verification on evaluators), and an explicit Rust developer-training/onboarding track. Cite this 2025 guide as the current 'state of the art' justification in TABOS's own memory-safety roadmap.

openQuestions: CRA harmonised standards for Annex III Class I/II products (the EN cyber-resilience standards being drafted by CEN/CENELEC) were not yet finalized as of fetch; confirm which specific standards/assurance levels TABOS must meet to self-certify the OS+hypervisor and avoid mandatory notified-body (module B/H) assessment before 11 Dec 2027.

Exact current signatory count and named memory-safety-roadmap publishers under the Secure by Design Pledge could not be enumerated (CISA signers page is JS-rendered; only 'over 200'/'hundreds' confirmed). Pull the live signers list if a precise count or specific OS/hypervisor-vendor signatories are needed.

Whether CISA issued a formal enforcement/expectation update after the 'publish a memory safety roadmap by the end of 2025' deadline passed (e.g., a 2026 Bad Practices v3.0) — not located in fetched sources; check for post-Jan-2026 CISA revisions.

No fetched US-gov source endorses a specific memory-safe crypto library for FIPS/compliance use; the Beyond Identity 'no certified memory-safe crypto exists' claim should be re-validated against 2025-2026 FIPS-140-3 validations of Rust crypto (e.g., aws-lc-rs) for TABOS's crypto stack decision.
DARPA TRACTOR maturity/results (post-2024 competition outcomes at ll.mit.edu/tractor) were not fetched; assess current C→Rust translation quality before relying on it for TABOS driver/engine porting.

findings.topic: C - seL4 as the verification gold standard for a microkernel
findings.summary: seL4 is ~10,000 lines of verified C. Its machine-checked Isabelle/HOL proof stack reaches DOWN TO THE BINARY (not just an abstract model), proving functional correctness plus security theorems (integrity, confidentiality/non-interference). seL4.systems claims ZERO functional-correctness defects in verified code since the proof completed in 2009 - 15+ years of deployment. Functional correctness 'implies, for free, the absence of whole classes of common programming errors such as buffer overflows, memory leaks' that 'usually plague C programs.' The proofs go beyond the most stringent existing certification levels: Common Criteria EAL7, ISO 26262 highest risk, DO-178C Level A (Isabelle/HOL not yet DO-178C-qualified but 'should be well able to meet DO-330 requirements'). Cited precedent: CompCert verified C compiler - Csmith found wrong-code bugs in every other compiler over 'six CPU-years' of fuzzing but NONE in CompCert's verified middle-end. Defect density in UNVERIFIED seL4 configs (SMP, x64 VT-x) is non-zero - verification, not the language, delivers the guarantee.
findings.designImplication: For a TABOS capability microkernel frozen at <=15 kSLOC holding the evaluators, verified C is the ONLY language with a binary-level functional-correctness proof for a production kernel in existence. If formal verification of the kernel core is ever a goal, C+seL4 (or building ON seL4 as the substrate) is the de-risked path - Rust has no comparable end-to-end kernel proof. C hire-ability and C-FFI are trivially the best of any option; toolchain (GCC/Clang/CompCert) is the most mature. Cost: C buys you NOTHING for free - safety comes only from the (expensive, ~20+ person-year-class) proof effort or from MISRA-grade discipline.

findings.topic: C - MISRA C:2023 as the industrial safety standard, and Unikraft is C
findings.summary: MISRA C is 'the de facto standard for developing software in C where safety, security and code quality are important,' originating in the 1994 UK automotive 'Development guidelines for vehicle based software' (predating ISO 26262 by ~10 years). Current edition is MISRA C:2023 (consolidates C:2012 plus amendments AMD1-4 and technical corrigenda; addenda subsequently issued). MISRA C++:2023 is also released, integrating the former AUTOSAR C++14 guidelines. (No standalone 'MISRA C:2025' main edition found as of fetch; 2023 is current.) Separately, Unikraft - the leading modular unikernel base - is overwhelmingly C: GitHub language bytes show 6,828,864 C vs 83,399 C++ and only 13,998 Rust, with Python/Perl/Awk/Make/KConfig tooling. Unikraft is single-address-space (no kernel/user split), uses a Linux-derived KConfig, and reports 1.7x-2.7x throughput vs Linux guests, ~1MB images, <10MB RAM, ~1ms boot on top of VMM time.
findings.designImplication: If TABOS builds its node image ON Unikraft, the libOS layer is C - the team must staff C and adopt a MISRA C:2023 + analyzer (Coverity/Polyspace/PC-lint) regime to get industrial safety, since plain C offers none. Building on Unikraft means inheriting a mature C unikernel ecosystem and KVM/Firecracker boot story essentially for free, at the cost of a C-centric codebase and KConfig build complexity. MISRA C:2023/C++:2023 are the citable standards to declare for any C/C++ layer in due-diligence and customer/regulator conversations.

findings.topic: C++ - Fuchsia/Zircon kernel, and Google's explicit per-language verdicts
findings.summary: Google's Zircon microkernel (the core of Fuchsia) is written in C++. Fuchsia's Programming Language Policy (last updated 2025-02-28) is an ALLOWLIST and evaluates each language explicitly: (1) C++ is 'approved for use THROUGHOUT the Fuchsia Platform Source Tree' (the only memory-unsafe-by-default language approved in-kernel), cons noted as weak async and memory-safety security bugs. (2) C: new uses 'discouraged' but approved for 'low-level systems programming, including within the kernel' and ABI-stable shared-library interfaces; C++ has stronger type safety ('recompiling some of our C code as C++ often results in compiler errors that surface latent bugs'). (3) Rust: approved throughout EXCEPT 'kernel. The Zircon kernel is built using a restricted set of technologies that have established industry track records of being used in production operating systems' - i.e. Rust is explicitly barred from the kernel as too unproven. (4) Go: 'not approved' except legacy netstack - Fuchsia had 'negative implementation experience,' Go components 'used more memory and kernel resources' than C++/Rust counterparts and 'toolchain produces large binaries.' Zircon targets ~100 syscalls.
findings.designImplication: C++ is the proven choice for a from-scratch production microkernel at Google scale, and is the ONLY memory-unsafe language Google trusts in-kernel today. The Fuchsia verdicts are a directly citable industrial precedent: for the TABOS frozen microkernel, C++ or C carry the 'production OS track record' that Google demanded; Rust does NOT yet clear that bar for THEM (notable given TABOS's Rust default). For userspace scheme-provider daemons and SDKs, Fuchsia approves Rust/C++ broadly - mirroring a split where the kernel stays conservative (C/C++/verified) and services can be Rust. C++ hire-ability and C-FFI are excellent; toolchain (Clang) world-class; the cost is the same memory-unsafety TABOS chose Rust to avoid.

findings.topic: Go - gVisor proves a GC'd kernel works in production at hyperscale
findings.summary: gVisor is an application kernel implementing a Linux-like interface, written in Go and running in userspace; its Sentry component is a full kernel (syscalls, memory management, page faulting, threading). 'Why Go': 'strong types, built-in bounds checks, no uninitialized variables, no use-after-free, no stack overflow, and a built-in race detector' - but 'the runtime often introduces performance overhead' and 'higher per-system call overhead.' Production users (gvisor.dev/users): Google runs 'millions of gVisor sandbox instances running daily' powering GKE Sandbox, Cloud Run, App Engine; ANTHROPIC uses gVisor 'to securely contain code execution within claude.ai' and is a regular contributor; also Cloudflare (Pages builds, 'a few seconds' container start 'near native performance'), DigitalOcean App Platform, Ant Group (at scale), Modal (fully on gVisor), Tailscale, Grist, Cloudflare. This is the strongest real-world case that a memory-safe GC'd language can host kernel-class isolation logic.
findings.designImplication: Go is a credible, battle-tested choice for the USERSPACE layers of TABOS - scheme-provider daemons (memory:, model:, tool:), protocol bridges (MCP/A2A), and orchestration - exactly the 'application kernel in userspace' niche gVisor occupies. Go's hire-ability is excellent, ecosystem vast, toolchain extremely mature, FFI-to-C via cgo workable (but cgo has call overhead and friction). It is NOT a kernel-core language (GC pauses, large binaries, runtime weight - confirmed by Fuchsia's rejection). gVisor/Anthropic precedent is also directly relevant to the WASM-nanoprocess/tool-sandbox security model TABOS needs.

findings.topic: Go - Biscuit (OSDI 2018) quantifies the HLL/GC tax for an in-kernel POSIX kernel
findings.summary: Biscuit (MIT, OSDI 2018, Cutler et al.) is a research POSIX kernel written in Go: ~28,000 lines of Go, 1,546 lines of assembly, NO C; implements 58 syscalls and two real device drivers (AHCI SATA, Intel 82599 Ethernet). Headline numbers: on kernel-intensive benchmarks (NGINX, Redis) the fraction of kernel CPU time spent on HLL features (GC + thread-stack-expansion checks) ranges 'up to 13%.' On nearly-identical syscall/page-fault/context-switch code paths written in both Go and C, 'the Go version was 5% to 15% slower' (C 5%-15% faster). Linux is 'up to 10% faster than Biscuit' on app benchmarks. GC detail: longest single GC pause for NGINX = 115 microseconds; longest summed GC delay to complete one NGINX request = 600 microseconds; 22% of NGINX requests delayed by collection, average delay 1.8us out of 45us request time. GCs consumed 1%-3% of total CPU at 18-48MB live heap. Function PROLOGUES (stack/GC-preempt checks), not GC itself, were 'the most expensive HLL feature.' Go's GC 'needs a factor of 2 to 3' of RAM headroom over live data. Paper explicitly notes 'a language such as Rust that avoids garbage collection might' do better.
findings.designImplication: Hard numbers for the kernel-vs-userspace language boundary: a GC'd HLL in-kernel costs roughly 5-15% on hot paths and up to 13% of kernel CPU, with sub-millisecond but real GC pauses and a 2-3x RAM tax. For a TABOS microkernel holding capability evaluators on the latency-critical path, this is the empirical justification for keeping the core in Rust/C/C++ (no GC) - Biscuit is the canonical citation that GC kernels are viable but pay a measurable, bounded tax. Conversely it bounds the COST of using Go for non-core services as modest (single-digit to ~13%), reinforcing Go for daemons where developer velocity > last-15%-of-perf.

findings.topic: Zig - immature (no 1.0), strong spatial-safety story, TigerBeetle as flagship
findings.summary: Zig has NO stable 1.0: per ziglang.org/download/index.json the latest releases are 0.16.0 (2026-04-13) and 0.15.2 (2025-10-11), with 0.17.0-dev master (2026-06-05) - still pre-1.0 with breaking changes every release (the std.Io interface visibly changed in 0.16). Safety model (ziglang.org/learn/overview): 580-line PEG grammar, 'no hidden control flow, no hidden memory allocations, no preprocessor, no macros'; four build modes (Debug/ReleaseSafe/ReleaseFast/ReleaseSmall) mixable to scope granularity; ReleaseSafe gives runtime checks for integer overflow, bounds, null, with @setRuntimeSafety(false) to opt out at hotspots; optionals replace null pointers; errors-as-values with try/errdefer. CRITICAL GAP vs Rust: Zig provides SPATIAL memory safety only - NO borrow checker, NO temporal safety (use-after-free/double-free not prevented by the type system). Flagship: TigerBeetle (financial OLTP DB) is written in Zig with a 'zero dependencies apart from the Zig toolchain' policy and TigerStyle (inspired by NASA's Power of Ten); it 'removes many instances of undefined behavior, provides spatial memory safety' and DESIGNS AWAY use-after-free/fragmentation via STATIC memory allocation (no dynamic alloc after startup) plus a VOPR deterministic simulator fuzzing on 1024 cores 24/7. Zig is also a drop-in C compiler, ships libc for 97 targets (~50MB tarball), and does C FFI via @cImport with no bindings - 'better at using C libraries than C is.'
findings.designImplication: Zig is the BEST C-FFI and cross-compilation story of any option (ships libc, is a C compiler, @cImport) - attractive for a layer that must wrap llama.cpp/C drivers. But for the TABOS frozen safety-critical microkernel, Zig is hard to justify in 2025-2026: no 1.0/stable spec, breaking changes each release, tiny hire-able talent pool, immature toolchain/ecosystem, and only spatial (not temporal) memory safety - so it does NOT match Rust's compile-time use-after-free guarantee. TigerBeetle shows Zig CAN reach extreme reliability, but only by adopting static allocation + a bespoke simulator + NASA-grade style - i.e. the safety comes from engineering discipline, not the language. Reasonable niche for TABOS: a C-shim/driver layer or tooling, not the capability kernel. Adopt TigerStyle/Power-of-Ten as an engineering standard regardless of language choice.

findings.topic: OCaml - MirageOS unikernels shipped in Docker Desktop (VPNKit)
findings.summary: MirageOS is a library OS that compiles OCaml into standalone unikernels running under Xen or KVM; code is developed on Linux/macOS then compiled to OS drivers for production. Properties (mirage.io): millisecond startup vs minutes for traditional OSes, few-MB self-contained binaries, few-MB memory footprint, fully event-driven 'with no support for preemptive threading.' Release cadence is slow/conservative: 4.0 (March 2022), 3.0 (Feb 2017), 2.0 (July 2014), 1.0 (Dec 2013) - infrastructure is self-hosted. Marquee production deployment: VPNKit (github.com/moby/vpnkit) - 'a set of tools and services for helping HyperKit VMs interoperate with host VPN configurations' - is written in OCaml (built via opam, `make ocaml`/`make depends`) and shipped inside Docker Desktop for Mac/Windows networking. Limits surfaced: cooperative event-driven model only (no preemptive threading), small talent pool, security advisories tracked (e.g. MirageOS advisory 03 on Solo5/Xen).
findings.designImplication: OCaml/MirageOS proves a memory-safe functional language can ship a unikernel-style network appliance in a mainstream consumer product (Docker Desktop) - directly analogous to TABOS's unikernel-style node image and scheme-provider daemons. Strengths: strong static types, GC, fast boot, tiny footprint, good for protocol/network logic (MCP/A2A bridges). Decisive weaknesses for TABOS: very small hire-able talent pool, niche ecosystem, no preemptive threading (awkward for a multi-tenant model: daemon), and OCaml-to-C FFI exists but is less ergonomic than Go/Rust. Best viewed as proof that the unikernel + memory-safe-HLL pattern is production-real, NOT as a primary TABOS implementation language given staffing risk.

findings.topic: Ada/SPARK - top certification pedigree, NVIDIA adoption, but ecosystem-bound
findings.summary: NVIDIA made the 'heretical decision to abandon C/C++ and adopt SPARK as its coding language of choice for security-critical software and firmware components' (root-of-trust/firmware). With SPARK NVIDIA can 'mathematically guarantee - and demonstrate to customers and regulators - a total absence of runtime errors in its most critical components and of unspecified behaviors in its root-of-trust applications,' giving 'the highest possible assurance against cyberattacks.' Scale signal via AdaCore RecordFlux/SPDM work: from a ~3,000-line protocol specification, ~135,000 lines of mathematically verifiable Ada SPARK code were generated (SPDM 1.1.0 subset). AdaCore positions SPARK as comparable to Rust for safer coding ('SPARK as good as Rust'), and SPARK/Ada carry decades of DO-178C / certification heritage. Ongoing: SPARK used to verify GPU-adjacent RISC-V firmware, 'narrowing language states to provably correct behavior.'
findings.designImplication: SPARK is the strongest off-the-shelf 'provable absence of runtime errors AND unspecified behavior' story short of full functional-correctness proof, and NVIDIA's defection from C/C++ is a powerful due-diligence precedent that memory-safe + provable beats C for security-critical firmware. For TABOS it loses on ECOSYSTEM and HIRING: the Ada/SPARK talent pool is tiny, tooling is largely single-vendor (AdaCore, commercial), library ecosystem is sparse, and FFI/interop with the broad LLM/WASM/cloud ecosystem (Python vLLM, C++ llama.cpp, Rust/Go services) is weak. Rational role: a candidate for the highest-assurance evaluator/root-of-trust component IF certification is pursued, not for the broad node image or service mesh. The lesson to bank: TABOS's Rust-default already captures most of SPARK's memory-safety win with vastly better hire-ability and ecosystem.

findings.topic: Cross-cutting - the industry pattern: conservative kernel core, memory-safe userspace
findings.summary: Across the serious greenfield-OS efforts, a consistent split emerges. Kernel CORE stays in a language with a 'production OS track record' or a proof: seL4 = verified C (~10kSLOC, binary-level proof, 0 defects/15yr); Zircon = C++ (Google bars even Rust from the kernel, citing 'restricted set of technologies with established industry track records'); Unikraft libOS = C. Memory-safe HLLs win in USERSPACE/application-kernel roles: gVisor = Go in userspace at Google/Anthropic/Cloudflare scale; MirageOS = OCaml unikernels in Docker Desktop. Quantified penalty for pushing a GC'd HLL into the kernel (Biscuit): up to 13% kernel CPU on HLL features, 5-15% slower hot paths, 2-3x RAM headroom, sub-ms GC pauses. Memory-safety WITHOUT GC is the contested middle: Rust (TABOS default, but unproven enough that Google excludes it from Zircon), Zig (spatial-only safety, no 1.0), SPARK (provable, tiny ecosystem).
findings.designImplication: TABOS's stated architecture already aligns with industry best practice: a frozen, non-GC, memory-safe-or-verified microkernel core, with GC'd/HLL freedom in userspace daemons and SDKs, and local LLM engines (Python/C++/CUDA) kept OUT of kernel behind drivers. Defensible language map per the evidence: kernel core = Rust (default) OR C/C++ if a verification or maximal-track-record argument dominates (seL4/Zircon precedent); userspace scheme daemons + bridges = Go or Rust (gVisor precedent); WASM tool runtime host = Rust/Go; driver/C-shim layer = Zig or C for best FFI. Engineering standards to ADOPT regardless of language: MISRA C:2023/C++:2023 for any C/C++; TigerStyle / NASA Power-of-Ten (static allocation, bounded loops, assertions) for the kernel; deterministic simulation testing (TigerBeetle VOPR) for the consensus/eval paths; and reserve formal methods (seL4 Isabelle/HOL or SPARK) for the smallest highest-assurance evaluator if certification is ever targeted.

openQuestions: Is TABOS building ON Unikraft (inheriting a C libOS + KConfig + MISRA-C obligation) or writing the node image from scratch in Rust? This single choice drives whether C/MISRA C:2023 becomes a first-class standard for the project.

Does TABOS intend to pursue any formal certification (Common Criteria EAL, DO-178C, ISO 26262) for the capability microkernel? If yes, verified C (seL4-style) or SPARK become serious contenders over Rust for the smallest evaluator core; if no, Rust's ecosystem/hiring advantage dominates.

What is the latency budget for the in-kernel capability evaluators? Biscuit's 5-15% HLL hot-path tax and sub-ms GC pauses define the threshold below which any GC'd language (Go/OCaml) is disqualified from the core.

Could TABOS adopt the gVisor/Anthropic model directly - reusing gVisor (Go) as the WASM/tool nanoprocess sandbox substrate rather than building a new runtime - given Anthropic already runs claude.ai code execution on gVisor?

Confirm exact MISRA edition to cite: MISRA C:2023 (consolidated) is current with addenda; no separate 'MISRA C:2025' main edition was found. Is the project conflating C:2023 with a planned/2025 addendum, and does the team also need MISRA C++:2023 (ex-AUTOSAR) if any C++ is used?

For the driver/C-shim layer wrapping llama.cpp/vLLM, is Zig's superior C-FFI and cross-compilation worth its pre-1.0 instability and spatial-only safety, versus plain C-with-MISRA or Rust-with-bindgen?

findings.topic: seL4 full functional verification: the canonical cost baseline (~20 person-years for ~10 kSLOC of C)
findings.summary: Klein et al., 'Comprehensive Formal Verification of an OS Microkernel' (ACM TOCS 32(1), Feb 2014) gives rigorously counted numbers: developing the seL4 kernel itself took 2.2 person-years (py); the functional-correctness proof took ~20 py total (Figure 19 total: 20.5 py), split into ~9 py for formal frameworks/tools/automation and 11 py for the seL4-specific proof (~8 py first refinement abstract-to-executable spec incl. invariants, ~3 py second refinement to C). Add-on proofs were dramatically cheaper once functional correctness existed: integrity/authority-confinement 7.4 person-months (10,500 lines Isabelle, done in 4 months); confidentiality (noninterference) 40.7 pm total (~5x integrity); translation-validation binary verification ~2 py; capDL refinement 17.2 pm. Verbatim cost figure: 'Our total cost per SLOC is $362 for the whole 22.7 py; it would be $127/SLOC for the estimated 8 py involved in replicating it for a new kernel... the industry rule-of-thumb for the lower EAL6 is already $1 k/LOC.' Kernel size today per seL4 FAQ (Apr 2025): verified RISC-V64 kernel ~10,000 SLOC; AArch32 12,100; AArch64 12,600 (with hyp); x64 16,000 SLOC.
findings.designImplication: Full seL4-style functional verification of a ~15 kSLOC TABOS microkernel in C+Isabelle is a 10-20 py program (multi-million-dollar, 3-5 calendar years with a small team) even with seL4's published playbook cutting replication cost to ~8 py / ~$127/SLOC. For a small team this is out of reach as a from-scratch effort; the realistic options are (a) adopt seL4 itself as the kernel and inherit its proofs, or (b) accept a weaker-but-cheap assurance tier (safe Rust + targeted proofs). Note also that proofs constrain design: seL4 keeps the kernel heap-free, event-based, big-lock — TABOS's frozen <=15 kSLOC budget is exactly the size class where verification is known feasible.

findings.topic: seL4 proof scope, maintenance model, and certification posture (2025 state)
findings.summary: seL4 whitepaper (rev 1.4, 2025-01-08) and FAQ: seL4 is still 'the first (and still only) general-purpose OS kernel with a full code-level functional correctness proof' across ARM, ARM_HYP, AARCH64, X64, RISCV64 configurations; on ARM(32) and RISC-V64 the proof extends to the binary (compiler removed from TCB) plus integrity/confidentiality security proofs. The 2009 proof was ~200,000 lines of Isabelle proof script; the actively maintained proof base is now 'well over a million lines' and is re-checked on every merge to master (commits that break proofs are rejected; proof status tracked in seL4/l4v and verification-manifest). Proof assumptions remain: assembly, boot code, TLB/cache management, hardware correctness, DMA off or trusted. Verification of MCS scheduling, AArch64 further properties, and multikernel/multicore configs is in progress, not done. The Verification page states the proofs 'go beyond what these certification schemes require' (Common Criteria, ISO 26262, DO-178C) at their most stringent levels.
findings.designImplication: If TABOS wants certification-grade kernel assurance without the 20-py bill, building the node image on seL4 (or mirroring its proof-maintenance discipline: proofs as CI gate, frozen kernel API) is the only proven path. Caveats relevant to TABOS architecture: multicore (SMP big-lock) and MCS configs are NOT yet verified, and the DMA assumption forces IOMMU/SMMU configs out of the verified set — a KVM/Firecracker-hosted unikernel deployment must check which guarantees survive its actual configuration. The 'proofs as per-commit CI' model is the engineering standard to copy even for unverified layers.

findings.topic: CertiKOS: verified concurrent kernel; layered Coq method cut marginal proof cost to <2 person-years
findings.summary: CertiKOS (Gu et al., OSDI'16): the mC2 kernel is 6,500 lines of C and x86 assembly, verified for contextual functional correctness in Coq, runs on stock x86 multicore and doubles as a hypervisor booting multiple Linux VMs. Verbatim: 'The entire proof effort for supporting concurrency took less than 2 person years'; it is 'the first proof of functional correctness of a complete, general-purpose concurrent OS kernel with fine-grained locking' (seL4's verified configs are/were unicore). The method is certified abstraction layers (deep specifications, per-layer contextual refinement) plus a certified compiler (CompCert lineage). The Yale FLINT publication list shows the kernel-verification line continued through Real-Time CertiKOS (POPL'20, verified preemptive scheduling/temporal isolation) and then pivoted toward verified compilation (CompCertO/CompCertELF/CompCertOC PLDI'25) and verified distributed/consensus protocols (LiDO PLDI'24/'25, Mysticeti S&P'26) — no newer full general-purpose kernel verification from the group since.
findings.designImplication: CertiKOS proves two things relevant to TABOS: (1) concurrency verification is possible but only with heavy layer discipline and a certified compiler; (2) the layered-refinement style makes incremental proofs cheap (<2 py for the concurrency increment) once the framework exists. If TABOS's kernel holds evaluators and must be concurrent, the seL4 route gives no verified SMP today; a CertiKOS-style layered design (or restricting the kernel to a verified-friendly event-loop/multikernel structure) is the architectural lever that keeps future verification possible.

findings.topic: Verus: deductive SMT verification for Rust is the active research standard for verified low-level systems (2024-2026)
findings.summary: Verus (verus-lang/verus, tagline 'Verified Rust for low-level systems code') statically proves user-written specs over executable Rust via SMT (Z3), supporting verification beyond the Rust type system (raw pointers, cells); it supports a subset of Rust, is 'under active development' with features 'broken and/or missing' per its own README — i.e., not a qualified or stable industrial tool. Its publications page documents heavyweight adoption in top-tier systems venues: Anvil (OSDI'24, Best Paper — verified liveness of Kubernetes-style controllers), VeriSMo (OSDI'24, Best Paper), OSDI'25 Distinguished Artifact, three USENIX Security'25 papers (one Distinguished Artifact), CortenMM (SOSP'25, Best Paper — verified memory management, from the Asterinas-adjacent group), AutoVerus (OOPSLA'25, Distinguished Artifact — LLM-generated Verus proofs), plus ICLR'25/ICML'25 work on automated proof generation, and ASPLOS'26 entries.
findings.designImplication: Verus is the strongest candidate for selective, property-targeted verification of TABOS's Rust kernel/daemon code (capability invariants, scheme-provider state machines, WASM sandbox boundary). It is research-grade: budget for toolchain churn, pin versions, and treat Verus proofs as high-value CI artifacts rather than certification evidence. The emerging LLM-assisted proof generation line (AutoVerus, ICLR/ICML'25) is directly relevant to an agent-native OS team: proof authoring cost is dropping.

findings.topic: VeriSMo (OSDI'24): concrete evidence that Rust+Verus collapses verification cost vs C+Isabelle — proof:code ~2:1, minutes-scale checking
findings.summary: VeriSMo (Zhou et al., Microsoft Research, OSDI'24) is 'the first verified security module for confidential VMs' on AMD SEV-SNP, written in Rust, verified with Verus for functional correctness, secure information flow, and VM confidentiality/integrity under a fully adversarial hypervisor. Key numbers from the paper: only 31 LOC of unsafe Rust for hardware primitives are trusted; 'the proof and specification annotations are approximately twice the size of the executable code for the implementation layer' (Table 2: core module 3,205 exec / 1,013 spec / 3,884 proof lines); whole verification re-runs in ~6 minutes on a 32-core machine (Verus+Z3 4.11.2); performance is comparable to a C implementation. Verification found 'a subtle requirement for VM confidentiality... overlooked by AMD SVSM' — a real flaw in the unverified industrial counterpart. Built by a ~6-person research team, layered into a hypervisor-interference machine model plus a permission/ownership-based implementation proof exploiting Rust's ownership for concurrency reasoning.
findings.designImplication: This is the cost datapoint that matters for TABOS: a security-critical privileged Rust component achieved seL4-class properties (functional correctness + information flow) at roughly 2:1 proof:code overhead and 6-minute CI verification — versus seL4's ~20:1 effort ratio and 11+ py in Isabelle over C. Rust's ownership model demonstrably does part of the separation-logic work for free. Recommendation: write TABOS's capability core in Verus-compatible Rust style from day one (minimize unsafe to a counted, audited primitive set — VeriSMo needed only 31 lines), even if proofs come later.

findings.topic: Kani (AWS): production-grade bounded model checking for Rust, monthly releases, but bounded and no concurrency
findings.summary: Kani Rust Verifier official docs: open-source model checker that 'automatically checks for many kinds of undefined behavior', particularly suited to unsafe blocks, plus panics (unwrap on None), arithmetic overflow, assertions and function contracts, via proof harnesses analogous to property-based tests. It either proves, disproves with a counterexample, or runs out of resources (bounded model checking — not unbounded proof in general). Releases monthly, synchronized with recent Rust nightly, 'generally up-to-date with the latest Rust language features'; explicit limitation: no concurrency support. The AWS verify-rust-std blog confirms Kani's positioning among peers: 'Kani supports both unsafe and safe code, but cannot guarantee unbounded verification in all cases.'
findings.designImplication: Kani is the lowest-friction formal tool a small team can adopt today: harnesses live next to unit tests, run in CI, and systematically cover unsafe blocks in TABOS daemons, the WASM host-call layer, and parser/serializer code (MCP/A2A protocol bridges are ideal targets). Adopt as a mandatory engineering standard: every unsafe block in TABOS ships with a Kani harness or a written justification. Do not rely on it for concurrency or unbounded properties — pair with Verus/loom there.

findings.topic: AWS 'Verify the Safety of the Rust Standard Library' initiative: scale of the unsafe-std problem and the crowd-sourced fix
findings.summary: AWS Open Source Blog (announced 2024): a Rust-Foundation-hosted, financially rewarded challenge contest to verify the Rust standard library. Hard numbers: 'the Rust standard library comprises roughly 35K functions, with approximately 7.5K unsafe functions, and 3K safe abstractions. The core library has 21K functions, with 7K unsafe functions and 1.7K safe abstractions. In the last 3 years, 57 soundness issues have been filed in the Rust standard library and 20 CVEs have been reported. Of these soundness issues, 28% of them were discovered in 2024.' Over 90% of Rust products depend on std; effort is tool-agnostic (Kani, Verus, Creusot, Aeneas, Gillian-Rust, Miri, Prusti, ESBMC listed as the verification landscape), works in a verify-rust-std fork with CI-integrated proofs, and AWS is 'working with the Rust language team to introduce function and loop contracts into the Rust mainline'. 20+ students/academics/researchers involved at posting time.
findings.designImplication: Two takeaways for TABOS: (1) the Rust std/core that TABOS userspace would link is NOT yet verified and has a real soundness-issue rate — for the kernel and nanoprocess runtime prefer no_std cores with a minimal, auditable dependency surface; (2) function/loop contracts are heading into mainline Rust, so writing contract-style specs in TABOS code now (Kani contracts / Verus specs) aligns with where the ecosystem is standardizing. The challenge repo is also a free corpus of verified-Rust idioms to copy.

findings.topic: Miri: dynamic UB detection used by the Rust Project itself — high bug yield, zero proof value
findings.summary: rust-lang/miri README: Miri interprets MIR and detects out-of-bounds/use-after-free, uninitialized data, intrinsic precondition violations, misalignment, type-invariant violations, data races with weak-memory emulation, memory leaks, and (experimental) Stacked/Tree Borrows aliasing violations. It is explicitly NOT a prover: 'Miri fundamentally cannot ensure that your code is sound'; it checks particular executions, and 'Miri does not catch every violation of the Rust specification in your program, not least because there is no such specification.' Documented real-world catches include bugs in std (VecDeque, Vec::append, BTreeMap, mpsc leak, RwLock::downgrade caught pre-landing) and major crates (TiKV, rand, crossbeam, regex, arc-swap, tokio-adjacent Mockall). Runs cross-target (e.g., big-endian s390x), deterministic with seed exploration (-Zmiri-many-seeds), single-threaded interpreter (slow), no FFI/networking.
findings.designImplication: Miri is a mandatory CI tier for all TABOS Rust code that compiles to a Tier-1 target (cargo miri test, plus a many-seeds soak job) — its catch list shows it finds exactly the unsafe-abstraction bugs an OS codebase will write. But it provides testing-grade, not certification-grade, evidence; and its own README is the cleanest citable confirmation that Rust still 'has no specification' in the formal sense Miri would need (relevant to the standards finding below). Budget for the interpreter slowdown by scoping Miri runs to unsafe-bearing crates.

findings.topic: RustBelt: the foundational soundness proof Rust's assurance story rests on (ERC project 2015-2021)
findings.summary: RustBelt (Derek Dreyer, MPI-SWS; ERC Consolidator Grant 2015, formally ended April 2021, 'continues in spirit') built the first machine-checked (Coq/Iris) semantic soundness foundations for Rust: 'RustBelt: Securing the Foundations of the Rust Programming Language' (POPL'18) gives formal tools for 'verifying safe encapsulation of unsafe code'; follow-ons include RustBelt Meets Relaxed Memory (POPL'20), Stacked Borrows aliasing model (POPL'20, implemented in Miri), GhostCell (ICFP'21), RefinedC (PLDI'21, Distinguished Paper+Artifact), and Ralf Jung's dissertation (2020 ACM Doctoral Dissertation Honorable Mention). The project page notes the original motivation verbatim: 'none of Rust's safety claims have been formally investigated, and it is not at all clear that they hold' — i.e., before RustBelt, Rust's safety was a conjecture; RustBelt proved the core type system plus key std libraries sound modulo a formal model.
findings.designImplication: For TABOS due diligence: Rust's memory-safety claim has machine-checked foundations (unlike C/C++ and unlike most 'safe' language marketing), including the critical property that correctly-encapsulated unsafe libraries preserve whole-program safety — this is the theoretical license for TABOS's 'small audited unsafe core + safe Rust everywhere else' strategy. But the model is of a Rust core calculus (lambda-Rust), not the real compiler/language, and the official aliasing model (Stacked vs Tree Borrows) is still experimental — TABOS unsafe code should be written to the conservative intersection and tested under both Miri modes.

findings.topic: Ferrocene: the qualified Rust toolchain — exact standards and dates (Oct 2023 -> Dec 2025)
findings.summary: Timeline from Ferrous Systems' own announcements: (1) Oct 2023 ('Officially Qualified - Ferrocene', certificate sent by TUV SUD Monday Oct 29 [2023]): Ferrocene 23.06.0 (Rust 1.68) became 'the first qualification of a Rust compiler' — ISO 26262 up to ASIL D and IEC 61508, announced then as 'qualified it at SIL4'; initial pricing €240/seat/year. (2) Jan 14, 2025: 'Ferrocene Becomes First Rust Toolchain to Achieve IEC 62304 Qualification' — Class C (medical), with Ferrocene 24.11 qualified on x86-64 Linux, Armv8-A bare metal, QNX Neutrino 7.1.0. (3) Dec 3, 2025: TUV SUD certified a subset of the Rust core library (libcore) to IEC 61508 SIL 2, shipped in Ferrocene 25.11.0 (Rust 1.89/1.90 base), targets incl. Armv7E-M and Armv8-A; named users Sonair (autonomous-robot ultrasonic sensor) and Kiteshield (mining safety, targeting SIL 3 components). Current ferrocene.dev wording: toolchain qualified ISO 26262 (ASIL D), IEC 61508 (SIL 3) with 'supports customer certification efforts for SIL 4', IEC 62304 (Class C); DO-178C (DAL C) 'support customer certification efforts' (not a qualification); certified core subset at ISO 26262 ASIL B / IEC 61508 SIL 2. Fully open source MIT/Apache-2.0 including qualification documents; 3-month release cycle tracking upstream rustc.
findings.designImplication: Rust now has an off-the-shelf, TUV-SUD-qualified compiler at the highest automotive level (ASIL D) plus a certified core-library subset — meaning a small TABOS team can buy compiler qualification (~€240-300/seat/yr) instead of spending person-years on tool qualification, something that has no C++ open-source equivalent and no counterpart at all for e.g. Zig or Go. Precision matters for claims: toolchain = ASIL D / SIL 3 / Class C qualified; SIL 4 and DO-178C are 'supported customer efforts'; std (not just core) is unqualified. If TABOS ever targets regulated deployments (medical agents, automotive), pin Ferrocene as the kernel/daemon toolchain and restrict kernel code to the certified core subset's API surface.

findings.topic: Rust specification status: FLS donated to the Rust Project (Mar 2025); official in-Project spec effort; NO ISO standard
findings.summary: Rust Blog, Mar 26, 2025 ('Adopting the FLS', by the Spec Team): the FLS — originally the 'Ferrocene Language Specification', assembled by Ferrous Systems and AdaCore and 'successfully used... to qualify toolchains based on Rust for use in safety-critical industries' — was adopted into the Rust Project 'as part of our ongoing specification efforts', facilitated by the Rust Foundation; the expansion of the acronym is being dropped (it's just 'the FLS'). The official specification path is defined by RFC 3355 and the Nov 15, 2023 'Our Vision for the Rust Specification' post: a Specification Team (subteam of lang team; editor role at the Rust Foundation — Joel Marcey), publishing as Rust Project documentation under Rust's own licensing, with releases coupled to the 6-week Rust release cadence; early versions 'derived heavily from... the Ferrocene specification'. The memory model / unsafe operational semantics is explicitly called 'still an open research area'. No ISO/IEC standardization process exists or is referenced anywhere in these documents; corroborating, the Miri README states plainly 'there is no such specification' for UB. Confirmed: Rust has no ISO standard; qualification today rides on the FLS + qualified toolchains, not on an international standard.
findings.designImplication: For TABOS's standards posture: do not wait for (or promise) an ISO-standardized Rust — it does not exist and is not the community's chosen route. The auditable artifacts that exist are: the FLS (now Project-owned, qualification-proven), the Reference, and Ferrocene's qualification documents. Engineering standard to adopt: declare the FLS edition + pinned toolchain version as TABOS's normative language baseline per release, and treat unsafe-code semantics as unsettled (conservative idioms, Miri/Tree-Borrows clean).

findings.topic: Safety-Critical Rust Consortium: founded June 12, 2024; coding guidelines (FLS-anchored) are the live deliverable
findings.summary: Rust Foundation press release, June 12, 2024: founding members are the Rust Foundation, AdaCore, Arm, Ferrous Systems, HighTec EDV-Systeme, Lynx Software Technologies, OxidOS, TECHFUND, TrustInSoft, Veecle, and Woven by Toyota. Scope 'may include the development of guidelines, linters, libraries, static analysis tools, formal methods and language subsets to meet industrial and legal requirements'; it coordinates with SAE JA1020 (existing safety-critical Rust effort), liaises with the Rust Project's Language Specification and Formal Methods teams, with public charter/minutes; quotes from Graydon Hoare and JF Bastien (Woven by Toyota). Its primary concrete output as of 2026: the Safety-Critical Rust Coding Guidelines (GitHub org Safety-Critical-Rust-Consortium), published/rendered online, built with Sphinx-Needs producing machine-parseable artifacts (needs.json, checksummed guideline IDs), and pinned against 'the current live version of the specification' (the FLS) via a spec.lock audit workflow — i.e., guidelines are formally traceable to FLS paragraphs, MISRA-style.
findings.designImplication: TABOS should adopt the Safety-Critical Rust Coding Guidelines as its in-repo coding standard now (they are free, FLS-traceable, and machine-checkable — amenable to CI linting), rather than inventing a house style or buying MISRA-C culture into a Rust codebase. The consortium's membership (Toyota, Arm, AdaCore, TrustInSoft) is the strongest available signal that industrial safety players are converging on Rust; aligning early means TABOS's engineering standards stay compatible with whatever certification profile emerges (incl. SAE JA1020).

findings.topic: Assurance levels a small team can realistically reach, per language (synthesis of fetched evidence)
findings.summary: Cost ladder assembled from the fetched numbers: (Tier 0, ~free) safe Rust + Miri in CI + Safety-Critical coding guidelines — catches the UB classes that dominate C kernel CVEs; RustBelt gives this tier formal foundations. (Tier 1, weeks) Kani harnesses on every unsafe block and protocol parser — bounded proofs, counterexamples, monthly-release tooling, no concurrency. (Tier 2, months-to-1-py per component) Verus deductive proofs of targeted invariants: VeriSMo demonstrates seL4-class properties (functional correctness + information flow) on a privileged Rust module at ~2:1 proof:code and 6-minute re-verification — feasible for a TABOS capability core of a few kSLOC. (Tier 3, 8-20+ py) seL4-style full functional verification of a C kernel in Isabelle: 20.5 py / $362 per SLOC ($127/SLOC replication estimate), vs $1k/LOC industry EAL6 rule of thumb; CertiKOS shows Coq layered method gets concurrency increments at <2 py but only atop years of framework. Toolchain qualification is orthogonal and purchasable only for Rust (Ferrocene ASIL D, ~€240/seat/yr) and for C via certified compilers — for C the proof tooling exists but at Tier-3 prices; for Rust Tiers 0-2 are uniquely cheap. No comparable verification or qualification stack was found for Go/Zig in any fetched source.
findings.designImplication: Recommended TABOS standard: kernel and scheme-provider daemons in Rust (Ferrocene-pinned, FLS-baselined, consortium guidelines, unsafe budget counted like VeriSMo's 31 LOC), with assurance tiers as CI gates — Miri+Kani mandatory, Verus proofs for the capability evaluator and WASM host boundary as a funded 1-py workstream. Reserve C only if adopting seL4 wholesale to inherit its proofs. Full bespoke functional verification of a new TABOS kernel is a Tier-3 (multi-year, multi-million) decision that should be explicitly deferred, with the architecture (small frozen kernel, layered services) kept verification-compatible so the option stays open.

openQuestions: seL4 AArch64: functional correctness to C exists, but which of binary verification and the security (integrity/confidentiality) proofs have landed for AArch64 by mid-2026? The FAQ snapshot says 'in progress' — matters if TABOS's node image targets Arm v8 under KVM.

Do certification assessors (TUV SUD et al.) accept Verus/Kani proof artifacts as verification evidence under ISO 26262 / IEC 61508 tool-confidence rules, or do they still require qualified static analyzers? No fetched source addresses assessor acceptance of SMT-based Rust proofs.

Ferrocene's IEC 61508 level is stated as SIL 4 in the Oct 2023 announcement but SIL 3 ('supports SIL 4 customer efforts') on the current site — was the qualification scope formally restated, and what exactly does the TUV SUD certificate say (TCL class vs SIL claim)? Needs the certificate text from TUV SUD's database.
